creator: zachary.crownover@gmail.com
text:
TLDR; Do not merge my patch, ignore it and close the ticket. The problem is solved.



Upon a brief first inspection I naively assumed that it was missing a library dependency for run time, and given that there are packages named 'future' within the versioned Python trees, it fed my misconception. When I went to test though, was when I realized something wasn't right.

I started with a negative test, ensure that with the current version, when building the package, is built fine. It was as expected, given the assumption was that this was a run time and not build dependency. I installed the created package and went to run the same commands, but I was not getting the same output. Something was off.

I reread the ticket and noticed I hadn't read closely enough, 'from future import standard_library', it read. The problem being it didn't say, '__future__'. I cloned the source for seafile-cli and grepped, that file wasn't there. After a little poking I realized it wasn't actually from there, it was from the FFI module in C for the RPC, libsearpc, and the Python binding which had done it incorrectly.

I checked the git log for the changes to the port and the last change in May had updated to 8.x, which had referenced a newer version of libsearpc which no longer included the issue, while the prior version, 7.x did in fact reference the broken version.


The problematic line, https://github.com/haiwen/libsearpc/commit/b0079d2a0c3ce0cb4698a162498b108244726912#diff-60f709d46c8836c44bfe85cda61b6ba6c6521d019be7768d817a6704c3c0b921L5 has since had its reference changed to be correct.

Given that, the patch is incorrect and not needed. The problem was fixed upstream and this port was fixed by updating to the newer version.
